paraphrase: The story of the Knight of the Laughing Tree is told by Meera Reed to Bran Stark in Book 3; Meera never names the crannogman, but it's implied he's a young Howland Reed, who traveled from the Neck to the Isle of Faces and then to Harrenhal for the tournament.

The tourney's attendees are given by title/heraldry rather than name: 'the king' Aerys Targaryen and his son 'the dragon prince' Rhaegar; 'the White Swords' (Kingsguard) welcoming a new brother (young Jaime Lannister); 'the storm lord' Robert Baratheon; 'the rose lord' Mace Tyrell; and Elia Martell, whom Rhaegar married instead of Cersei Lannister.
Tywin Lannister did not attend the tourney, reportedly angry at Aerys both for taking Jaime into the Kingsguard and for refusing to marry Rhaegar to Cersei.

Howland Reed was attacked by three young squires while walking across a field, mocked as a 'mud-man' and 'frog-eater,' and beaten and cursed until a 'she-wolf' (Lyanna Stark) drove the squires off with a blunted sword and took him to her tent to treat his injuries.

At the Stark tent, Howland met Lyanna's brothers: 'the wild wolf' Brandon Stark (~19), 'the quiet wolf' Eddard Stark (~18), and 'the pup' Benjen Stark (~14); Lyanna was about fifteen and Howland about sixteen.

spoken_quote: a song so sad it made
paraphrase: That night at the feast Prince Rhaegar sang 'a song so sad it made' Lyanna cry — presented as the first hinted connection between Rhaegar and Lyanna.
verbatim: “Under Harren’s roof he ate and drank with the wolves, and many of their sworn swords besides, barrowdown men and moose and bears and mermen. The dragon prince sang a song so sad it made the wolf maid sniffle, but when her pup brother teased her for crying she poured wine over his head. A black brother spoke, asking the knights to join the Night’s Watch. The storm lord drank down the knight of skulls and kisses in a wine-cup war. The crannogman saw a maid with laughing purple eyes dance with a white sword, a red snake, and the lord of griffins, and lastly with the quiet wolf . . . but only after the wild wolf spoke to her on behalf of a brother too shy to leave his bench.

spoken_quote: a maid with laughing purple eyes
paraphrase: At the feast, Ashara Dayne ('a maid with laughing purple eyes') danced with a Kingsguard, Oberyn Martell, Jon Connington, and Ned Stark, after Brandon asked her on Ned's behalf because Ned was too shy to ask himself.
verbatim: “Under Harren’s roof he ate and drank with the wolves, and many of their sworn swords besides, barrowdown men and moose and bears and mermen. The dragon prince sang a song so sad it made the wolf maid sniffle, but when her pup brother teased her for crying she poured wine over his head. A black brother spoke, asking the knights to join the Night’s Watch. The storm lord drank down the knight of skulls and kisses in a wine-cup war. The crannogman saw a maid with laughing purple eyes dance with a white sword, a red snake, and the lord of griffins, and lastly with the quiet wolf . . . but only after the wild wolf spoke to her on behalf of a brother too shy to leave his bench.

paraphrase: Rumors hold that Ned or Brandon got Ashara Dayne pregnant; a year later she had a stillborn child, her brother Arthur Dayne was killed by Ned and Howland, and Ashara committed suicide, so the truth may never be known.
Later at the feast, Howland spotted the three squires who had attacked him; each served a knight, from House Haigh, House Blount, and House Frey respectively.
The Starks suggested Howland win back his honor by beating the offending knights in the tournament, but Howland felt he wasn't good at riding and jousting and feared he'd just make a fool of himself.
That night Howland went out by the lake, looked toward the Isle of Faces, and prayed to the old gods of the north.

A mystery knight with name and face hidden, identified only by a weirwood tree with a laughing face painted on his shield, appeared at the tourney, defeated the Haigh, Blount, and Frey knights at jousting, and returned their horses and armor only after they punished their squires for attacking Howland — restoring Howland's honor to the crowd's cheers.

spoken_quote: all they ever found was [the Knight's] painted shield, hanging … in a tree
paraphrase: King Aerys, paranoid, saw the Tree Knight as an enemy and sent his son Rhaegar to find out who he was, but 'all they ever found was [the Knight's] painted shield, hanging ... in a tree.'
The Knight is described as 'short of stature' and clad in 'ill-fitting armor made up of bits and pieces' — details that could fit the small Howland if he'd had to scrounge armor together to compete.
Meera's story suggests the old gods or the green men of the Isle of Faces heard Howland's prayer by the lake and 'gave strength to his arm' so he could win back his honor.
verbatim: “Whoever he was, the old gods gave strength to his arm. The porcupine knight fell first, then the pitchfork knight, and lastly the knight of the two towers. None were well loved, so the common folk cheered lustily for the Knight of the Laughing Tree, as the new champion soon was called. When his fallen foes sought to ransom horse and armor, the Knight of the Laughing Tree spoke in a booming voice through his helm, saying, ‘Teach your squires honor, that shall be ransom enough.’ Once the defeated knights chastised their squires sharply, their horses and armor were returned. And so the little crannogman’s prayer was answered . . . by the green men, or the old gods, or the children of the forest, who can say?”

paraphrase: ASX argues Game of Thrones isn't a fairytale world where the weak beat the strong through righteousness alone — citing Littlefinger's duel loss to Brandon Stark as proof pluck doesn't overcome skill — and that the old gods aren't shown as benevolent, prayer-answering deities but are more associated with blood sacrifice, making magical intervention for Howland inconsistent with the series' established rules of magic.

Bran himself concludes within the story that the Knight was Howland, but ASX argues this is suspicious because ASOIAF mysteries typically present an obvious answer first that turns out to be wrong (e.g., Jon Snow believed to be Ned Stark's bastard but actually Lyanna's son; Jon Arryn believed poisoned by the Lannisters but actually poisoned by Lysa for Littlefinger) before revealing a more complex truth.
When Meera tells the story, Jojen seems surprised that Bran hasn't already heard it from his father Ned, suggesting Ned or someone close to him may have been the mystery knight.
Benjen, Ned, and Brandon were all trained to ride and fight, so unlike Howland they wouldn't have needed divine intervention to plausibly win a few jousts.
Brandon was described as 'hot-blooded' and rash, a 'gallant fool' — a personality that fits someone who would defend Howland's honor by jousting in disguise.
Brandon was 'tall,' which conflicts with the Knight's 'short of stature' description, a problem for identifying him as the mystery knight.
It doesn't make sense for Brandon to have competed in disguise as a mystery knight, since he later jousts openly as himself in the same tournament.
Ned was younger and shorter than Brandon, so he might better fit the Knight's short stature.
Ned riding for Howland in disguise could help explain how Howland became such a loyal, lifelong friend to Ned.

spoken_quote: a certain distaste for the … pageantry of tourneys
paraphrase: Ned is described as 'solemn' and 'somber,' responsible and reserved, with 'a certain distaste for the ... pageantry of tourneys' — not a good match for the showy, theatrical persona of a mystery knight bearing a 'laughing' tree shield.
Benjen was fourteen or younger at the time, fitting the Knight's short stature, and he was the one who offered Howland armor and a horse to compete, showing enthusiasm for someone avenging him.
At fourteen, Benjen was very young, and there's no suggestion in the text that he was particularly skilled at riding, fighting, or jousting, making him an unlikely candidate.
As a teenage girl, Lyanna could fit the Knight's short stature and 'ill-fitting armor.'
Lyanna had strong motivation to help Howland, since she was the one who initially rescued him from the squires.
Lyanna is described as 'wild,' 'willful,' and 'boyish,' and compared to Arya Stark — fighting to help a friend fits that personality, as it would fit Arya's.

Lyanna being the Knight would add a twist of the assumed-male mystery knight turning out to be a girl; Meera describes the Knight's voice 'booming' through the helm, which could reflect the helmet's echo disguising Lyanna's voice.
It's questionable whether a fifteen-year-old girl would be strong enough to use a lance in a joust.

In an unreleased Winds of Winter preview chapter, another fifteen-year-old girl, Elia Sand, jousts so often she's nicknamed 'Lady Lance' — offered as precedent that a girl that age could plausibly joust.

Roose Bolton says that Lyanna 'was half a horse,' and in the same statement says 'A great jouster must be a great horseman' — directly linking riding skill to jousting ability and implying Lyanna could have done well in a tourney.
verbatim: “For the moment. I had another, once. Domeric. A quiet boy, but most accomplished. He served four years as Lady Dustin’s page, and three in the Vale as a squire to Lord Redfort. He played the high harp, read histories, and rode like the wind. Horses … the boy was mad for horses, Lady Dustin will tell you. Not even Lord Rickard’s daughter could outrace him, and that one was half a horse herself. Redfort said he showed great promise in the lists. A great jouster must be a great horseman first.”

paraphrase: Jaime Lannister thinks that jousting is three-quarters horsemanship, reinforcing that Lyanna's renowned riding ability would translate into jousting skill.

King Aerys specifically sent Rhaegar to find and unmask the Knight of the Laughing Tree; later in the same tournament Rhaegar publicly crowned Lyanna Queen of Love and Beauty, and later ran off with her, sparking Robert's Rebellion and eventually leading to the birth of Jon Snow — ASX suggests the Rhaegar-Lyanna relationship may have begun when Rhaegar was sent to find the Knight.

spoken_quote: maybe some words were whispered in a tent of a night
paraphrase: ASX speculates Rhaegar may have found and identified Lyanna as the Knight but chose to keep her secret, impressed by her boldness, beginning their romance: 'maybe some words were whispered in a tent of a night,' 'Words or kisses, maybe more.'

Harrenhal (where Rhaegar later 'abducts' Lyanna) is close to the Isle of Faces; fan theories hold that Rhaegar and Lyanna secretly married on the Isle of Faces among the weirwoods, which would let psychic Bran later witness the wedding in a vision to confirm R+L=J.

A 'crazier' alternative theory some fans hold: Bran Stark, using future greensight, warged into past-Howland at the moment of his prayer, adopting the mystery-knight identity with a weirwood-painted shield and using old-gods magic to win the jousts and restore Howland's honor — paralleling Bran's Season 6 breaking of Hodor's mind by reaching into his past self to make him 'hold the door.'

This Bran-warging theory fits some of the talk of magic in Meera's story and would fulfill Bran's wish, expressed after hearing the tale, to be a knight for a day: Bran thinks 'A day would be enough.'
verbatim: Bran shook his head. The day was growing old by then, and long shadows were creeping down the mountainsides to send black fingers through the pines. If the little crannogman could visit the Isle of Faces, maybe I could too. All the tales agreed that the green men had strange magic powers. Maybe they could help him walk again, even turn him into a knight. They turned the little crannogman into a knight, even if it was only for a day, he thought. A day would be enough.

paraphrase: ASX ultimately judges the Bran time-travel theory 'kinda ridiculous' despite its thematic resonance, and concludes it was probably Lyanna Stark who was the Knight of the Laughing Tree.
